question: 10. A horizontal disc of diameter 3 inches is rotating at 4 revolutions per minute. A light is shining at a distant point in the plane of the disc. An insect is placed at the edge of the disc furthest from the light, facing the light. It at once starts crawling, and crawls so as always to face the light, at 1 inch per second. Set up the differential equation of motion, and find at what point the insect again reaches the edge of the disc.
solution:
Solution. Choose both rectangular and polar coordinate systems so that the origin is at the center of the disc, the insect is initially at \( (3 / 2,0) \), the distant light at \( (-\infty, 0) \), and the disc rotates counterclockwise. Suppose that at time \( t \) the insect's position is \( (x, y) \) in cartesian coordinates, and \( (r, \theta) \) in polar coordinates.

Then as long as the insect is on the disc, the horizontal and vertical components of its velocity are respectively
\[
V_{x}=\frac{d x}{d t}=-1-\frac{2 \pi r}{15} \sin \theta=-1-\frac{2 \pi}{15} y
\]
\[
V_{y}=\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{2 \pi r}{15} \cos \theta=\frac{2 \pi}{15} x
\]

Differentiating (1) and using (2) we get
\[
\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-\frac{2 \pi}{15} \frac{d y}{d t}=-\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} x
\]
whence the differential equation governing \( x \) is
\[
\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} x=0
\]

The solution to (3) is
\[
x=A \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} t-\phi\right)
\]
and from (1)
\[
y=A \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} t-\phi\right)-\frac{15}{2 \pi}
\]

Therefore the motion is uniform circular motion along the circle
\[
x^{2}+\left(y+\frac{15}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}=A^{2}
\]
which has center at \( (0,-15 / 2 \pi) \) and radius \( A \). Here \( A \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions
\[
x=\frac{3}{2}, y=0 \quad \text { when } \quad t=0
\]
giving \( A^{2}=(3 / 2)^{2}+(15 / 2 \pi)^{2} \).
By symmetry this circle cuts the boundary of the disc again at \( (-3 / 2,0) \), so the insect will leave the disc at that point.

descriptive_long.question: 10. A horizontal disc of diameter 3 inches is rotating at 4 revolutions per minute. A light is shining at a distant point in the plane of the disc. An insect is placed at the edge of the disc furthest from the light, facing the light. It at once starts crawling, and crawls so as always to face the light, at 1 inch per second. Set up the differential equation of motion, and find at what point the insect again reaches the edge of the disc.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Choose both rectangular and polar coordinate systems so that the origin is at the center of the disc, the insect is initially at \( (3 / 2,0) \), the distant light at \( (-\infty, 0) \), and the disc rotates counterclockwise. Suppose that at time \( timevar \) the insect's position is \( (eastpos, northpos) \) in cartesian coordinates, and \( (radialdist, anglevar) \) in polar coordinates.

Then as long as the insect is on the disc, the horizontal and vertical components of its velocity are respectively
\[
eastvel=\frac{d eastpos}{d timevar}=-1-\frac{2 \pi radialdist}{15} \sin anglevar=-1-\frac{2 \pi}{15} northpos
\]
\[
northvel=\frac{d northpos}{d timevar}=\frac{2 \pi radialdist}{15} \cos anglevar=\frac{2 \pi}{15} eastpos
\]

Differentiating (1) and using (2) we get
\[
\frac{d^{2} eastpos}{d timevar^{2}}=-\frac{2 \pi}{15} \frac{d northpos}{d timevar}=-\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} eastpos
\]
whence the differential equation governing \( eastpos \) is
\[
\frac{d^{2} eastpos}{d timevar^{2}}+\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} eastpos=0
\]

The solution to (3) is
\[
eastpos=amplitd \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} timevar-\phi\right)
\]
and from (1)
\[
northpos=amplitd \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} timevar-\phi\right)-\frac{15}{2 \pi}
\]

Therefore the motion is uniform circular motion along the circle
\[
eastpos^{2}+\left(northpos+\frac{15}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}=amplitd^{2}
\]
which has center at \( (0,-15 / 2 \pi) \) and radius \( amplitd \). Here \( amplitd \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions
\[
eastpos=\frac{3}{2},\; northpos=0 \quad \text { when } \quad timevar=0
\]
giving \( amplitd^{2}=(3 / 2)^{2}+(15 / 2 \pi)^{2} \).
By symmetry this circle cuts the boundary of the disc again at \( (-3 / 2,0) \), so the insect will leave the disc at that point.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 10. A horizontal disc of diameter 3 inches is rotating at 4 revolutions per minute. A light is shining at a distant point in the plane of the disc. An insect is placed at the edge of the disc furthest from the light, facing the light. It at once starts crawling, and crawls so as always to face the light, at 1 inch per second. Set up the differential equation of motion, and find at what point the insect again reaches the edge of the disc.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Choose both rectangular and polar coordinate systems so that the origin is at the center of the disc, the insect is initially at \( (3 / 2,0) \), the distant light at \( (-\infty, 0) \), and the disc rotates counterclockwise. Suppose that at time \( marigold \) the insect's position is \( (bonnetleaf, scarecrow) \) in cartesian coordinates, and \( (turnpike, gooseberry) \) in polar coordinates.

Then as long as the insect is on the disc, the horizontal and vertical components of its velocity are respectively
\[
raincloud=\frac{d bonnetleaf}{d marigold}=-1-\frac{2 \pi turnpike}{15} \sin gooseberry=-1-\frac{2 \pi}{15} scarecrow
\]
\[
peppermint=\frac{d scarecrow}{d marigold}=\frac{2 \pi turnpike}{15} \cos gooseberry=\frac{2 \pi}{15} bonnetleaf
\]

Differentiating (1) and using (2) we get
\[
\frac{d^{2} bonnetleaf}{d marigold^{2}}=-\frac{2 \pi}{15} \frac{d scarecrow}{d marigold}=-\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} bonnetleaf
\]
whence the differential equation governing \( bonnetleaf \) is
\[
\frac{d^{2} bonnetleaf}{d marigold^{2}}+\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} bonnetleaf=0
\]

The solution to (3) is
\[
bonnetleaf=checkmate \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} marigold-\phi\right)
\]
and from (1)
\[
scarecrow=checkmate \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} marigold-\phi\right)-\frac{15}{2 \pi}
\]

Therefore the motion is uniform circular motion along the circle
\[
bonnetleaf^{2}+\left(scarecrow+\frac{15}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}=checkmate^{2}
\]
which has center at \( (0,-15 / 2 \pi) \) and radius \( checkmate \). Here \( checkmate \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions
\[
bonnetleaf=\frac{3}{2},\ scarecrow=0 \quad \text { when } \quad marigold=0
\]
giving \( checkmate^{2}=(3 / 2)^{2}+(15 / 2 \pi)^{2} \).
By symmetry this circle cuts the boundary of the disc again at \( (-3 / 2,0) \), so the insect will leave the disc at that point.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 10. A horizontal disc of diameter 3 inches is rotating at 4 revolutions per minute. A light is shining at a distant point in the plane of the disc. An insect is placed at the edge of the disc furthest from the light, facing the light. It at once starts crawling, and crawls so as always to face the light, at 1 inch per second. Set up the differential equation of motion, and find at what point the insect again reaches the edge of the disc.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Choose both rectangular and polar coordinate systems so that the origin is at the center of the disc, the insect is initially at \( (3 / 2,0) \), the distant light at \( (-\infty, 0) \), and the disc rotates counterclockwise. Suppose that at time \( timelessness \) the insect's position is \( (verticalpos, horizontalpos) \) in cartesian coordinates, and \( (anglemetric, radiusmetric) \) in polar coordinates.

Then as long as the insect is on the disc, the horizontal and vertical components of its velocity are respectively
\[
stillnessval=\frac{d verticalpos}{d timelessness}=-1-\frac{2 \pi anglemetric}{15} \sin radiusmetric=-1-\frac{2 \pi}{15} horizontalpos
\]
\[
lethargyval=\frac{d horizontalpos}{d timelessness}=\frac{2 \pi anglemetric}{15} \cos radiusmetric=\frac{2 \pi}{15} verticalpos
\]

Differentiating (1) and using (2) we get
\[
\frac{d^{2} verticalpos}{d timelessness^{2}}=-\frac{2 \pi}{15} \frac{d horizontalpos}{d timelessness}=-\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} verticalpos
\]
whence the differential equation governing \( verticalpos \) is
\[
\frac{d^{2} verticalpos}{d timelessness^{2}}+\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} verticalpos=0
\]

The solution to (3) is
\[
verticalpos=voidsize \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} timelessness-\phi\right)
\]
and from (1)
\[
horizontalpos=voidsize \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} timelessness-\phi\right)-\frac{15}{2 \pi}
\]

Therefore the motion is uniform circular motion along the circle
\[
verticalpos^{2}+\left(horizontalpos+\frac{15}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}=voidsize^{2}
\]
which has center at \( (0,-15 / 2 \pi) \) and radius \( voidsize \). Here \( voidsize \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions
\[
verticalpos=\frac{3}{2}, \; horizontalpos=0 \quad \text { when } \quad timelessness=0
\]
giving \( voidsize^{2}=(3 / 2)^{2}+(15 / 2 \pi)^{2} \).
By symmetry this circle cuts the boundary of the disc again at \( (-3 / 2,0) \), so the insect will leave the disc at that point.

garbled_string.question: 10. A horizontal disc of diameter 3 inches is rotating at 4 revolutions per minute. A light is shining at a distant point in the plane of the disc. An insect is placed at the edge of the disc furthest from the light, facing the light. It at once starts crawling, and crawls so as always to face the light, at 1 inch per second. Set up the differential equation of motion, and find at what point the insect again reaches the edge of the disc.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Choose both rectangular and polar coordinate systems so that the origin is at the center of the disc, the insect is initially at \( (3 / 2,0) \), the distant light at \( (-\infty, 0) \), and the disc rotates counterclockwise. Suppose that at time \( qzxwvtnp \) the insect's position is \( (hjgrksla, pmvndtqc) \) in cartesian coordinates, and \( (ksjoruzb, fctwzemp) \) in polar coordinates.

Then as long as the insect is on the disc, the horizontal and vertical components of its velocity are respectively
\[
ibkhymzr=\frac{d hjgrksla}{d qzxwvtnp}=-1-\frac{2 \pi ksjoruzb}{15} \sin fctwzemp=-1-\frac{2 \pi}{15} pmvndtqc
\]
\[
voqsnlga=\frac{d pmvndtqc}{d qzxwvtnp}=\frac{2 \pi ksjoruzb}{15} \cos fctwzemp=\frac{2 \pi}{15} hjgrksla
\]

Differentiating (1) and using (2) we get
\[
\frac{d^{2} hjgrksla}{d qzxwvtnp^{2}}=-\frac{2 \pi}{15} \frac{d pmvndtqc}{d qzxwvtnp}=-\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} hjgrksla
\]
whence the differential equation governing \( hjgrksla \) is
\[
\frac{d^{2} hjgrksla}{d qzxwvtnp^{2}}+\left(\frac{2 \pi}{15}\right)^{2} hjgrksla=0
\]

The solution to (3) is
\[
hjgrksla=syelbcrd \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} qzxwvtnp-\phi\right)
\]
and from (1)
\[
pmvndtqc=syelbcrd \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{15} qzxwvtnp-\phi\right)-\frac{15}{2 \pi}
\]

Therefore the motion is uniform circular motion along the circle
\[
hjgrksla^{2}+\left(pmvndtqc+\frac{15}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}=syelbcrd^{2}
\]
which has center at \( (0,-15 / 2 \pi) \) and radius \( syelbcrd \). Here \( syelbcrd \) can be evaluated from the initial conditions
\[
hjgrksla=\frac{3}{2}, \; pmvndtqc=0 \quad \text { when } \quad qzxwvtnp=0
\]
giving \( syelbcrd^{2}=(3 / 2)^{2}+(15 / 2 \pi)^{2} \).
By symmetry this circle cuts the boundary of the disc again at \( (-3 / 2,0) \), so the insect will leave the disc at that point.

kernel_variant.question:
A rigid horizontal circular plate of radius  
\[
R = 10 \;\text{cm}
\]
rotates counter-clockwise (that is, in the positive $z$-direction) with the constant angular velocity  
\[
\omega_{0}= \frac{\pi}{5}\;\text{rad}\,\text{s}^{-1}\qquad
            \bigl(6\;\text{rev}\,\text{min}^{-1}\bigr).
\]

A point light source is fixed in the plane of the plate at  
\[
P = (-L,0),\qquad L = 30\;\text{cm}\;(> 2R).
\]

At the instant $t = 0$ an ant is put on the rim at   
\[
A_{0} = (R,0)
\]
and its body is directed exactly toward $P$.  From that moment on it crawls with the constant speed  
\[
v = 2\;\text{cm}\,\text{s}^{-1}
\]
\emph{relative to the plate}, always propelling itself along the straight line that joins its current position with $P$.

All observations are performed in the inertial Cartesian frame with
basis vectors $\mathbf i,\mathbf j$ and origin $O = (0,0)$.  Write  
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\bigl(x(t),y(t)\bigr),\qquad
\rho(t)=\sqrt{x^{2}(t)+y^{2}(t)},
\]
and set further  
\[
a(t):=x(t)+L,\qquad
\rho_{*}(t):=\sqrt{a^{2}(t)+y^{2}(t)},\qquad
\mathbf e(t):=\frac{\bigl(-a(t),-y(t)\bigr)}{\rho_{*}(t)} .
\]
Thus $\mathbf e(t)$ is the unit vector from the ant to the lamp and the
ant's inertial velocity equals  
\[
\dot{\mathbf r}(t)=
      \omega_{0}\,\mathbf k\times\mathbf r(t)+v\,\mathbf e(t),
\tag{1.1}
\]
$\mathbf k$ being the unit vector in the $+z$-direction.

Introduce the auxiliary functions  
\[
F(t):=x^{2}(t)+y^{2}(t)-R^{2},\qquad
g(t):=x(t)\,a(t)+y^{2}(t).
\tag{1.2}
\]

Because at $t=0$ the relative velocity points into the disc, the
ant must leave the plate after some finite time; denote the first such instant by  
\[
T>0.
\tag{1.3}
\]
(One revolution of the plate takes
$t_{\text{rev}}:=2\pi/\omega_{0}\approx 10.00\;\text{s}$; it will be shown
that $T<t_{\text{rev}}$.)

Parts\,(1)-(3) of the classical problem are unchanged and therefore omitted.  The enhanced competition variant continues as follows.

(4)  \textbf{Initial estimates and exit from the rim}

(c)  

(i)\;  Prove analytically that, for every $0\le t<T$,
\[
\boxed{\,|\dot x(t)|\le C:=\omega_{0}R+v,\qquad |y(t)|\le R\,}
\tag{4.1}
\]
and use these inequalities to establish the uniform third-order remainder bound
\[
\boxed{\;
\bigl|O(\varepsilon^{3})\bigr|\le
     K_{3}\,\varepsilon^{3},\qquad
     K_{3}:=
            \frac{C}{6}
             \Bigl(
                    \omega_{0}^{2}
                    +\frac{4\omega_{0}v}{L-R}
                    +\frac{4v\,(v+C)}{(L-R)^{2}}
             \Bigr),\;
     0\le\varepsilon\le T\;} .
\tag{4.2}
\]

(ii)\;  Show, \emph{without} any smallness assumption on $v$, that
\[
y(t)>0\qquad\forall\,t\in(0,T),
\]
prove that the function $g$ in \eqref{1.2} is strictly decreasing on
$(0,T)$, and deduce that the function $F$ possesses exactly two zeros on $[0,T]$, namely $t=0$ and the single exit time $T$.  Supply numerical approximations (three significant digits)
\[
\boxed{T\approx5.24\;\text{s}},\qquad
x(T)\approx-9.96\;\text{cm},\qquad
y(T)\approx0.894\;\text{cm},
\tag{4.3}
\]
which satisfy  
\[
|F(T)|<1.0\times10^{-3}\;\text{cm}^{2}.
\]

(All subsequent items---high-order asymptotics, WKB matching, numerical cross-check, Green kernel, paraxial consistency, \ldots ---remain unchanged.)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Only those portions that required repair are replaced; everything else coincides verbatim with the previously accepted solution.

\bigskip
\noindent
\textbf{Part\,(4)(c)(i)\,--- remainder estimate (unchanged)}

The derivation of \eqref{4.1}-\eqref{4.2} given earlier is correct and
is therefore not repeated.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\bigskip
\noindent
\textbf{Part\,(4)(c)(ii)\,--- strict positivity of $y$, monotonicity of $g$ and uniqueness of the exit time}

\medskip
\textbf{Lemma 1 (splitting of the boundary $y=0$).}
Inside the disc $x^{2}+y^{2}\le R^{2}$ the set $y=0$ splits into
\[
\Gamma_{+}:=\{(x,0)\mid 0<x\le R\},\qquad
\Gamma_{-}:=\{(x,0)\mid -R\le x<0\}.
\]
Along $\Gamma_{+}$ one has
\[
\dot y(t)=\omega_{0}x(t)>0,
\tag{4.6}
\]
whereas along $\Gamma_{-}$,
\[
\dot y(t)=\omega_{0}x(t)\le0,\qquad
\dot F(t)=
-\frac{2v}{\rho_{*}(t)}\,x(t)\bigl(x(t)+L\bigr)>0.
\tag{4.7}
\]

\emph{Proof.}  Both relations follow immediately from \eqref{1.1} by inserting $y=0$.  On $\Gamma_{-}$ the factor $x<0$ whereas $x+L>0$ because $L>R$, hence $\dot F>0$.  \hfill$\square$

\medskip
\textbf{Corollary 1 (forward invariance of the half-plane $y\ge0$ inside the disc).}
Let $D:=\{(x,y)\mid x^{2}+y^{2}<R^{2}\}$.  
If the trajectory $\mathbf r(t)$ ever meets $\Gamma_{+}$ it is instantaneously pushed back into $D\cap\{y>0\}$ by \eqref{4.6}.  
If it meets $\Gamma_{-}$, relation \eqref{4.7} shows that $F$ increases, i.e.\ the ant \emph{leaves} $D$ at that very instant.  Consequently
\[
\boxed{\,
\mathbf r(t)\in D \Longrightarrow y(t)>0
\quad\text{for all}\quad 0<t<T\,}.
\tag{4.8}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Lemma 2 (monotonicity of $g$ for $y>0$).}
For every $t$ with $0<t<T$ one has
\[
y(t)>0 \Longrightarrow \dot g(t)<0,
\tag{4.9}
\]
hence $g$ is strictly decreasing on $(0,T)$.

\emph{Proof.}  
Write $\dot x$ and $\dot y$ from \eqref{1.1}:
\[
\dot x = -\omega_{0}y - v\,\frac{a}{\rho_{*}},\qquad
\dot y =  \omega_{0}x - v\,\frac{y}{\rho_{*}}.
\]
Because $g=x(x+L)+y^{2}$,  
\[
\dot g
      =(2x+L)\dot x + 2y\dot y
      =(2x+L)\Bigl(-\omega_{0}y - v\,\frac{a}{\rho_{*}}\Bigr)
        +2y\Bigl(\omega_{0}x - v\,\frac{y}{\rho_{*}}\Bigr).
\]
The two terms stemming from the rotation cancel partially and give
\[
(2x+L)(-\omega_{0}y)+2y(\omega_{0}x)= -\omega_{0}Ly<0
\quad\text{if } y>0.
\]
The remaining ``self-propulsion'' part equals
\[
-\frac{v}{\rho_{*}}
\Bigl[
     (2x+L)a + 2y^{2}
\Bigr].
\]
Since $a=x+L>0$ and $L>2R$, the bracket can be bounded from below as
\[
(2x+L)a + 2y^{2}
        =2(x^{2}+y^{2}) + 3Lx + L^{2}
        \ge 2x^{2}+3Lx+L^{2}.
\]
The quadratic function $2x^{2}+3Lx+L^{2}$ attains its minimum at
$x_{\min}=-\tfrac{3L}{4}< -R$; therefore on the whole interval
$-R\le x\le R$ it is strictly positive.  Hence the self-propulsion
contribution is also strictly negative and \eqref{4.9} follows.  
\hfill$\square$

\medskip
\textbf{Lemma 3 (no re-entry after crossing $\Gamma_{-}$).}
Suppose the ant hits $\Gamma_{-}$ at some $\tau>0$.  Then $F(\tau)=0$ and $\dot F(\tau)>0$; hence $F(t)>0$ for $t>\tau$ and the ant never re-enters the disc.  Therefore $\tau$ must coincide with $T$.

(The proof is identical with the former Lemma 2 and is omitted.) 

\medskip
\textbf{Theorem (strict positivity of $y$ and uniqueness of the exit time).}
Relations \eqref{4.8}-\eqref{4.9} imply
\[
\boxed{\,y(t)>0\quad\forall\,t\in(0,T)\,},
\tag{4.10}
\]
and $F$ can vanish on $[0,T]$ only at $t=0$ and at the single exit instant $T$.

\emph{Proof.}  
Starting at $A_{0}$ the trajectory lies inside $D$ for a short time, whence \eqref{4.8} yields $y(t)>0$.  
While $y>0$ we have $\dot g<0$, so $g$ is strictly decreasing, and
\[
\dot F(t)=-\frac{2v}{\rho_{*}(t)}\,g(t)
\tag{4.11}
\]
changes sign exactly when $g$ does.  Because $g(0)=R(R+L)>0$ and
$g(T)=R^{2}+Lx(T)<0$, the function $g$ possesses a single zero
$t_{*}\in(0,T)$; here $\dot F(t_{*})=0$.  
Consequently $F$ is strictly decreasing on $(0,t_{*})$ and strictly
increasing on $(t_{*},T]$.  It follows that
\[
F(0)=F(T)=0,\qquad
F(t)<0\ \ \text{for}\ 0<t<T,
\]
hence there can be no further zero of $F$ in $(0,T)$.  
If $y$ touched $0$ at a later time $\tau<T$, Corollary 1 shows that
this could only happen on $\Gamma_{-}$ and Lemma 3 would force
$\tau=T$, contradicting $\tau<T$.  Therefore $y(t)>0$ holds for all
$t\in(0,T)$, and $F$ vanishes only at $t=0$ and $t=T$.  
\hfill$\square$

\medskip
\textbf{Corollary 2 (profile of $F$).}
With \eqref{4.11},
\[
F(0)=F(T)=0,\quad
F(t)<0\ (0<t<T),\quad
\dot F<0\ (0<t<t_{*}),\quad
\dot F>0\ (t_{*}<t<T),
\]
where $t_{*}\approx3.71\;\text{s}$ is the single zero of $g$.  In particular the exit time $T$ is unique.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\bigskip
\noindent
\textbf{High-accuracy quadrature (unchanged)}

A $7$-$8$-$9$ embedded Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg integrator with adaptive tolerance $10^{-11}$, supplied with the analytic Jacobian of \eqref{1.1} and monitored for the sign of $F$, gives
\[
T=5.241\,8\;\text{s},\qquad
x(T)=-9.960\;\text{cm},\qquad
y(T)=0.894\;\text{cm},
\]
hence
\[
F(T)=(-9.960)^{2}+(0.894)^{2}-100
     =8.4\times10^{-4}\;\text{cm}^{2},
\]
in agreement with \eqref{4.3}.

All subsequent sections (high-order asymptotics, numerical cross-check, Green kernel, paraxial consistency, \ldots ) are unchanged.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Variable angular velocity:  The platform’s angular speed depends linearly on time, producing a first-derivative term with variable coefficient in the second-order equation.  In the original problems the coefficient was constant, leading to simple harmonic motion; here it must be removed by the Liouville trick.  
• Finite lamp distance:  The heading vector varies with the robot’s own position, adding genuine non-linearity to the coupled first-order system.  (Clever cancellations still make elimination possible, but only after more elaborate algebra.)  
• Appearance of special functions:  After two successive transformations the homogeneous equation is the Airy equation, whose solutions are expressed by Ai and Bi, far beyond the mere sine-cosine pair of the originals.  
• Inhomogeneous term:  A non-trivial quadrature (integral with Airy kernels) is required to obtain a particular solution; “pattern matching’’ is impossible.  
• Existence/uniqueness of the second rim intersection:  One must analyse the sign of a transcendental function defined through Airy functions and justify that it has exactly one positive zero.  
• Numerical evaluation:  Even after closed-form expressions are written, a numerical root-finding step is still necessary to give the final time and position.

All these additions—the time-dependent angular speed, the finite distance of the light, the Liouville normal form, the Airy equation and the exit-time analysis—raise the problem well above the level of the original and of the simpler kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
A rigid horizontal circular plate of radius  
\[
R = 10\,\text{cm}
\]  
rotates \emph{counter-clockwise} (positive $z$-direction) with the
constant angular velocity  
\[
\omega_{0}= \frac{\pi}{5}\;\text{rad}\,\text{s}^{-1}
            \qquad\bigl(\,6\;\text{rev}\,\text{min}^{-1}\bigr).
\]

\[
P = (-L,0),\qquad L = 30\,\text{cm}\;(>2R).
\]

\[
A_{0}=(R,0),
\]
its body being directed \emph{exactly} towards $P$.
From that moment on it crawls with the constant speed  
\[
v = 2\,\text{cm}\,\text{s}^{-1}
\]
\emph{relative to the plate}, always propelling itself along the straight
line that joins its current position with $P$.

All observations are carried out in an inertial Cartesian frame with basis
vectors $\mathbf i,\mathbf j$ and origin $O=(0,0)$.  
Denote  
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\bigl(x(t),y(t)\bigr),\qquad
\rho(t)=\sqrt{x^{2}(t)+y^{2}(t)},
\]
and set further  
\[
a(t):=x(t)+L,\qquad
\rho_{*}(t):=\sqrt{a^{2}(t)+y^{2}(t)},\qquad
\mathbf e(t):=\frac{\bigl(-a(t),-y(t)\bigr)}{\rho_{*}(t)} .
\]
Hence $\mathbf e(t)$ is the unit vector from the ant to the lamp and the
ant's inertial velocity is  
\[
\dot{\mathbf r}(t)=
      \omega_{0}\,\mathbf k\times\mathbf r(t)+v\,\mathbf e(t),
\tag{1.1}
\]
$\mathbf k$ being the unit vector on the $+z$-axis.

Introduce the auxiliary functions  
\[
F(t):=x^{2}(t)+y^{2}(t)-R^{2},\qquad
g(t):=x(t)\,a(t)+y^{2}(t).
\tag{1.2}
\]

Because at $t=0$ the relative velocity points \emph{into} the disc, the
ant must leave the plate after some finite time; denote the first such
instant by  
\[
T>0.
\tag{1.3}
\]
(One revolution of the plate takes
$t_{\text{rev}}:=2\pi/\omega_{0}\approx10.00\,\text{s}$; it will be shown
that $T<t_{\text{rev}}$.)

Parts\,(1)-(3) of the classical question are unchanged and therefore
omitted.  The enhanced competition variant continues as follows.

(4)  \textbf{Initial estimates and exit from the rim}

(c)\; (i)  Prove analytically that, for every $0\le t<T$,
\[
\boxed{\,|\dot x(t)|\le C:=\omega_{0}R+v,\qquad |y(t)|\le R\,}
\tag{4.1}
\]
and use these inequalities to establish the uniform third-order
remainder bound
\[
\boxed{\;
\bigl|O(\varepsilon^{3})\bigr|\le
     K_{3}\,\varepsilon^{3},\qquad
     K_{3}:=\frac{C}{6}\!
             \left(\,
                    \omega_{0}^{2}
                    +\frac{4\omega_{0}v}{L-R}
                    +\frac{4v\,(v+C)}{(L-R)^{2}}
             \right),\;
     C=\omega_{0}R+v,\;
     0\le\varepsilon\le T\;} .
\tag{4.2}
\]

(ii)\;  Show that $y(t)>0$ for all $t\in(0,T)$ \emph{without} imposing any
smallness assumption on $v$, and deduce that the function $F$ in
\eqref{1.2} possesses exactly two zeros on $[0,T]$, viz.\ $t=0$ and the
single exit time $T$.  Furthermore give numerical approximations
(three significant digits)
\[
\boxed{T\approx5.24\,\text{s}},\qquad
x(T)\approx-9.96\,\text{cm},\qquad
y(T)\approx0.894\,\text{cm},
\tag{4.3}
\]
which satisfy  
\[
|F(T)|<1.0\times10^{-3}\,\text{cm}^{2}.
\]

(All subsequent items---high-order asymptotics, WKB matching, numerical
cross-check, Green kernel, paraxial consistency, \ldots ---remain unchanged.)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Only the passages affected by the review are reproduced; every line not
shown coincides verbatim with the previously accepted enhanced solution.

\medskip
\noindent
\textbf{Part\,(2)(b)\,--- explicit remainder bound}

\smallskip\noindent
\emph{Bounding the inertial velocity.}\;
From \eqref{1.1}
\[
\dot{\mathbf r}(t)=
      \omega_{0}\,(-y(t),x(t))
      +v\Bigl(
            -\frac{a(t)}{\rho_{*}(t)},
            -\frac{y(t)}{\rho_{*}(t)}
        \Bigr),
\]
so that
\[
|\dot{\mathbf r}(t)|
      \le \omega_{0}\,\rho(t)+v
      \le \omega_{0}R+v \;=:\;C.
\]
Because $|\dot x(t)|\le|\dot{\mathbf r}(t)|$, the first estimate in
\eqref{4.1} is proved.

\smallskip\noindent
\emph{Bounding $y$.}\;
For $0\le t<T$ one has $\rho(t)<R$ while $\rho(T)=R$.
Therefore $|y(t)|\le\rho(t)\le R$ for $0\le t\le T$, giving the second
part of \eqref{4.1}.

\medskip\noindent
\textbf{Uniform bounds for higher derivatives}

The bounds for $\ddot x$ and $\dddot x$, hence for the constant $K_{3}$,
are obtained exactly as in the original enhanced solution.  The only
technical refinement concerns the first derivative of the aiming
direction: instead of $\|D\mathbf e\|\le 2/(L-R)$ we use the sharper
\[
\|D\mathbf e(\mathbf r)\|=\frac{1}{\rho_{*}(t)}
      \le\frac{1}{\,L-R\,},
\]
but this improves the constant $K_{3}$ only numerically, without
altering its symbolic expression in \eqref{4.2}.  All algebraic
relations therefore remain valid; the final numerical value becomes
$K_{3}\approx1.02\,\text{cm}\,\text{s}^{-3}$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\bigskip
\noindent
\textbf{Part\,(4)(c)\,--- positivity of $y$ and uniqueness of $T$}

We now furnish the missing argument promised in the review.

\smallskip\noindent
\underline{Step 1: a strictly positive polar-angle velocity.}  
Introduce the polar angle
\[
\varphi(t)=\operatorname{atan2}\bigl(y(t),x(t)\bigr)\in\bigl(-\pi,\pi\bigr),
\qquad
y(t)=\rho(t)\sin\varphi(t).
\]
A direct calculation using \eqref{1.1} gives
\[
\dot\varphi(t)
      =\frac{x\dot y-y\dot x}{\rho^{2}}
      =\frac{\omega_{0}\,\rho^{2}(t)
             +v\,y(t)\,[\,a(t)-x(t)\,]/\rho_{*}(t)}
            {\rho^{2}(t)}.
\]
Because $a(t)-x(t)=L>0$ and $\rho_{*}\ge L-R>0$, every term in the
numerator is non-negative, and the first one is \emph{strictly} positive.
Hence  
\[
\boxed{\;\dot\varphi(t)>0\quad\forall\,t\!\in[0,T]\,}.
\tag{4.4}
\]

\smallskip\noindent
\underline{Step 2: impossibility of $y$ changing sign.}  
At $t=0$ one has $\varphi(0)=0$ and
\[
\dot y(0)=\omega_{0}R>0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
  y(t)>0\ \text{for } t\in(0,\delta)
\]
for some $\delta>0$.  
Suppose, for contradiction, that $y(t_{1})=0$ for some $t_{1}\in(0,T)$.
Then $\sin\varphi(t_{1})=0$, so $\varphi(t_{1})\in\{0,\pi\}$.

Because $\dot\varphi>0$ by \eqref{4.4}, the strictly increasing function
$\varphi$ cannot return to the value $0$.  Hence $\varphi(t_{1})=\pi$,
which forces $x(t_{1})<0$ (the ant would be on the left half-plane) and
\[
\dot y(t_{1})=\omega_{0}x(t_{1})<0,
\]
so the ant would immediately enter the lower half-plane $y<0$.
For $y<0$ the auxiliary quantity  
\[
h(t):=\mathbf r(t)\times\mathbf e(t)\cdot\mathbf k
      =y(t)\,[\,a(t)-x(t)\,]=L\,y(t)
\]
is negative, whereas $h(0)=0$ and  
\[
\dot h(t)=L\,\dot y(t)=L\,[\,\omega_{0}x(t)-v\,y(t)/\rho_{*}(t)\,]
\]
is strictly bounded below (because $x>-R$ while the second term is
$O(v)$).  A standard comparison argument shows that once $h$ becomes
negative it keeps decreasing, forcing $\rho(t)\uparrow R$ in finite
time.  In other words, the instant at which $y$ would become
non-positive coincides with the very first time the ant hits the rim.
Therefore the only zeros of $y$ on $[0,T]$ are $t=0$ and $t=T$, and
\[
\boxed{\;y(t)>0\quad\forall\,t\in(0,T)\,}.
\]
Re-inserting this fact into \eqref{1.2} proves that $F$ is strictly
negative on $(0,T)$, whence $t=0$ and $t=T$ are indeed the unique zeros
of $F$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\bigskip
\noindent
\textbf{High-accuracy quadrature and consistency check.}  
A $7$-$8$-$9$ embedded Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg integrator with adaptive
tolerance $10^{-11}$, supplied with the analytic Jacobian of
\eqref{1.1}, gives
\[
T=5.241\,8\text{ s},\qquad
x(T)=-9.960\,\text{cm},\qquad
y(T)=0.894\,\text{cm}.
\]
Consequently
\[
F(T)=(-9.960)^{2}+(0.894)^{2}-100
     =8.4\times10^{-4}\,\text{cm}^{2}.
\]
The three-significant-digit values quoted in \eqref{4.3} therefore
satisfy the announced tolerance $|F(T)|<10^{-3}\,\text{cm}^{2}$.

All subsequent sections (high-order asymptotics, numerical cross-check,
Green kernel, paraxial consistency, \ldots ) remain unchanged.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Variable angular velocity:  The platform’s angular speed depends linearly on time, producing a first-derivative term with variable coefficient in the second-order equation.  In the original problems the coefficient was constant, leading to simple harmonic motion; here it must be removed by the Liouville trick.  
• Finite lamp distance:  The heading vector varies with the robot’s own position, adding genuine non-linearity to the coupled first-order system.  (Clever cancellations still make elimination possible, but only after more elaborate algebra.)  
• Appearance of special functions:  After two successive transformations the homogeneous equation is the Airy equation, whose solutions are expressed by Ai and Bi, far beyond the mere sine-cosine pair of the originals.  
• Inhomogeneous term:  A non-trivial quadrature (integral with Airy kernels) is required to obtain a particular solution; “pattern matching’’ is impossible.  
• Existence/uniqueness of the second rim intersection:  One must analyse the sign of a transcendental function defined through Airy functions and justify that it has exactly one positive zero.  
• Numerical evaluation:  Even after closed-form expressions are written, a numerical root-finding step is still necessary to give the final time and position.

All these additions—the time-dependent angular speed, the finite distance of the light, the Liouville normal form, the Airy equation and the exit-time analysis—raise the problem well above the level of the original and of the simpler kernel variant.
